questions.text: Who is the author of 'The Summit Within'?
questions.explanation: Major Ahluwalia was a member of the first successful Indian expedition to Mount Everest in 1965.

questions.text: What was the author's dominant feeling as he stood on the summit of Everest?
options: Humility and a sense of smallness
questions.explanation: Looking at the vast panorama, he felt humble and slightly sad that the climb was over.

questions.text: According to the author, why is climbing a mountain more than just a physical feat?
options: It is a spiritual and emotional experience
questions.explanation: He believes the conquest of the summit is also a conquest of the self.

questions.text: What does the 'summit within' refer to in the chapter?
questions.explanation: It refers to one's own internal struggles, fears, and personal growth.

questions.text: Why did the author choose to climb Everest?
options: Because it was near his home
Because it is the highest, mightiest and has defied many efforts
Because his friends told him to
questions.explanation: He found Everest's challenge irresistible and full of obstacles.

questions.text: What qualities are required to climb a mountain, according to Ahluwalia?
questions.explanation: The demonstration of these physical and mental qualities is exhilarating.

questions.text: What do climbers do when they share the same rope?
options: One climber cuts the steps while the other belays (holds firm)
questions.explanation: Climbing is a communal effort where team members support each other's physical progress.

questions.text: What 'relic' of his faith did Major Ahluwalia leave on Everest?
questions.explanation: He left a picture of Guru Nanak as a mark of reverence.

questions.text: What did Rawat (the author's companion) leave on the summit?
questions.explanation: He left a picture of the Goddess Durga.

questions.text: What did Phu Dorji leave on Everest?
questions.explanation: He left a relic of the Buddha.

questions.text: What did Edmund Hillary bury under a cairn at the summit?
questions.explanation: Hillary had left a cross on the mountain during his historic 1953 climb.

questions.text: A 'cairn' is:
options: A heap of rocks and stones
questions.explanation: Climbers use cairns to mark locations or bury items.

questions.text: The experience of having climbed to the summit changes you ________.
questions.explanation: The author claims the experience provides a life-long change to the person's soul.

questions.text: Which summit is more difficult to climb than Everest, according to the author?
options: The internal summit of the mind
questions.explanation: He believes self-conquest is the most daunting and rewarding task.

questions.text: What was the feeling of 'sadness' at the summit?
options: Because there was no more higher to climb
questions.explanation: He felt a tinge of regret because the 'ultimate' goal had been reached and all roads would now lead down.

questions.text: The author says that a man who has been to the mountain top is:
questions.explanation: The transformation is permanent and profound.

questions.text: Climbing is a communal act because it requires:
options: Cooperation and shared effort between partners
questions.explanation: Famous climbers have left records of help given by others; no one climbs alone.

questions.text: What does 'panorama' mean?
options: A wide, unbroken view of a whole region
questions.explanation: The author viewed the vast scenery of the Himalayas from the top.

questions.text: Ahluwalia's story is an account of his expedition in:
questions.explanation: The 1965 Indian expedition was a landmark in Indian mountaineering.

questions.text: What does the author gain from his internal summit?
questions.explanation: Climbing the 'internal mountain' helps one face life's ordeals with more strength.

questions.text: The climb to the summit of the mind is ________ to the climb to the Everest summit.
questions.explanation: The author draws a parallel between physical and spiritual achievements.

questions.text: Mountains are nature at its ________.
questions.explanation: He sees mountains as a supreme means of communion with God.

questions.text: Why do people risk their lives to climb mountains, according to the author?
options: Because it is part of human nature to overcome obstacles
questions.explanation: Man takes delight in overcoming obstacles that are difficult.

questions.text: When the author reached the summit, he felt he was ________.
questions.explanation: The scale of the mountain reminded him of his own physical limitations.

questions.text: The 'ordeals' of life mentioned in the end of the chapter mean:
questions.explanation: His experience as an 'Everester' helped him face life's hardships resolutely.

questions.text: What is the physical act of climbing a mountain like?
options: A struggle with ice and rock
questions.explanation: The text describes the grim struggle and the physical effort involved in every step.

questions.text: How many successful Indian expeditions to Everest occurred before 1965?
questions.explanation: The 1965 expedition was the first successful one for India.

questions.text: The summit of Everest is a place of ________ according to the symbols left there.
questions.explanation: All items left behind were symbols of devotion and not of conquest.

questions.text: Ahluwalia mentions that 'even when the mountain is not Everest', every climb is ________.
questions.explanation: He values the act of climbing itself as a teacher.

questions.text: Which mountain range is Everest part of?
questions.explanation: Everest is the highest peak in the Himalayan range.

questions.text: What does 'belay' mean in mountaineering?
options: To fix a rope to a person or rock to prevent a fall
questions.explanation: It is a vital safety technique mentioned in the text.

questions.text: The term 'panoramic' refers to the view from:
questions.explanation: The wide view of the surrounding peaks from the top of Everest.

questions.text: The author felt that the spiritual effect of the climb was more ________ than the physical one.
questions.explanation: The memory of the summit remains vivid in his mind forever.

questions.text: Major Ahluwalia's tone in the chapter is:
questions.explanation: He spends more time discussing the inner feelings than the technical aspects of the climb.

questions.text: What does he say about the obstacles on a mountain?
options: They help you enjoy the climb more
They are a supreme challenge to one's physical powers
questions.explanation: Overcoming obstacles provides a sense of victory.

questions.text: Which word best describes the 'summit within'?
questions.explanation: It is the understanding of one's own character and spirit.

questions.text: The author concludes that 'Everester' is someone who has ________.
options: An enriched soul and changed perspective
questions.explanation: The label implies a person who has seen the world from the highest perspective.

questions.text: What did the author say about the other peaks visible from Everest?
options: They looked like a jewelled necklace around the neck of the summit
questions.explanation: He describes the beauty of the surrounding peaks as a royal decoration.

questions.text: The physical act of climbing to the summit of a mountain on this earth is a ________ act.
questions.explanation: The text contrasts the physical, visible act with the internal, spiritual one.

questions.text: What is the most important lesson the author wants readers to learn?
options: Conquer the internal summit to face life's challenges
questions.explanation: The central theme is that internal growth is as important as external achievement.
